# Mike Masinick's Website!

## Welcome to my website! Here you will find a summary of my analysis, graphs, and a conclusion from my EX09 Notebook. 

## Summary: 
My analysis examines whether of not the COMP 110 course structure should use more gradual explainations for complex topics such as classes and objects. I did this by analyzing how this years students programming experience related to percieved difficulty and their support for additional resources. 

For my analysis, I looked at three key outcomes and grouped students by their coding experience. THe three key outcomes were average difficulty ratings, percentage of students reporting high difficulty (ratings ≥ 4), and support for pre-lecture videos.

The first graph I created was looked at perception of difficulty compared to students coding experience. In this graph, we can see a clear downward trend in percieved difficulty as experience increases. Students with no coding experience reported the highest difficulty at approximately 4.5, where as students with over two years of coding experience reported at about a 2.3. This graph suggests a strong negative relationship between prior experience and perceived difficulty, which can be interpreted as beginners being disproportionately challenged by the course material. 

My second graph looked at experience vs. support for pre-lecture videos. I again grouped students by peior experience, but this time, I averaged their responsed about pre lecture videos. The graph shows that all students generally support pre-lecture videos, but support is strongest from students with less experience coding. This suggests that beginners could especially benefit from having extra materials before class so they may preview difficult materials before having them introduced in class. 

Graph 3 builds on the first graph by showing the percentage of students in each group that rated the classes difficulty a 4 or higher out of 7. Instead of looking at averages, this graph shows how many students in each group are having a hard time. The results show more than 80% of beginners rated the course as highly difficult, while fever than 20% of students with two or more years of experience did so. This points out a very noticible difference between beginners and more experienced students. 

Overall, these graphs show that programming experience has a large effect on percieved difficulty of the course. The data supports that the course should introduce more gradual explainations, especially for challanging topics. Since beginners are both struggling more and showing more support for per-lecture videos, I think adding more learning resources would help make the course less overwhelming for beginners. This is especially important in this course because of how many different backgrounds students are coming from.

## Conclusion

These graphs clearly show that prior experience plays a large role in how students percieve the course. Beginners are more likely to report higher difficulty and are far more likely to struggle. They also show more support for additional resources such as pre-lecture videos. This suggests that the current course curriculum may not meet the needs of less experienced students. 

In order to fix this, I believe adding more gradual explainations and additional support on the website would be beneficial for less experienced students. However, it is important to keep more experienced students engaged at the same time. The evidence supports making the course more accessible without slowing down progress too much. I recommend more walkthrough videos that are availible to quiz prep and explainations that are more readily availible on the website.
